author: @rogerbarton497
text: Brilliant.  With my Heath Robinson hat on, have a long large diameter sloping/vertical pipe, with a water valve at the bottom and two valves at the top, 1 for air 1 for water.  Fill the pipe with water (rain stream &c) via the top water valve.  When full close the water valve at the top and open the air valve and bottom water valve.  Air will be dawn in as the pipe empties, so connect your bellows machine to the air valve and you have mechanical power.  This would be good for small water flows as the water is stored in the pipe until it's full.

author: @nigelwilliams7920
text: On your home block you no doubt have a wee stream with a bit of fall. So you could easily make a trompe to give you a bit of nice compressed air. Pressure depending on available height difference below the water intake. You could then reticulate that air using a garden hose or similar to where your air engine is located.  Of course a low head water wheel would certainly make better use of the stream’s energy resource but hey were playing with ideas here.

author: @tonyponsford
text:
Hi Rob, nice work and i like where its leading us to. Many years ago i used to read a science magazine, who's name alludes me, but in one issue there was a double-skinned house, that moderated the living space temperature by using a thermal store/sink below the house...reminds me where you might be heading here. 
Whilst helping to install double glazed panels into a conservatory, i had to place them on the scaffold, and to prevent scratching them, lay them on a thick sheet of polystyrene...even clouds did not prevent a rapid increase of temperature, almost burning hot... maybe your cheap passive heat source ?
Regards
Tony

replies.author: @tonyponsford
replies.text: The magazine was Omni, i believe.

replies.author: @cherylm2C6671
replies.text: A family in Norway did that: a 2 story house and (extra) garden was enclosed in a geodesic skinned in agricultural poly. Trombe walls also accomplish this.

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
The valve apparatus could be done with 3 copper "T"s and off the shelf copper pipe and solder... or even PVC if it's not getting high temperatures.
Robert was using what he had available to make a prototype, but aluminum pipe is not that easy to come by and using 2 different metals has its own issues (not the least of which is cost due to supply and demand economics)

author: @kenrowe167
text: If you're using a vacuum then, even if you pull a perfect vacuum, the maximum force operating on your system can be no more than 1 atmosphere x area of piston head. This was the drawback with the original condensing steam engines. That's why all more modern engines use positive pressure.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: You can use positive pressure in a vacuum steam system it just has to be a closed loop.

replies.author: @kenrowe167
replies.text: @Barskor1 Give me an example of such a system

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Finally a use for the camshaft from the Nissan! At the beginning of the video I was thinking of s seesaw motion but a camshaft motion like this makes do much more sense. Imagine a funnel with a large intake from the wind to compress it to create a fast motion. Attach magnets to the bellow shaft and have them quickly move past coils as well as fan generators to store energy into a super capacitor bank or a battery. 
This is just absolutely awesome Robert, it could ventilate 5 patients for the energy cost of currently ventilating 1. On top of the energy harvesting you have something bloody special here.
